name: Silicon Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Silicon. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for Silicon surface cleaning without substrate damage

wavelength.description: Near-IR wavelength with good Silicon absorption for contaminant removal

spotSize.description: Beam spot diameter for precise cleaning with adequate energy density

repetitionRate.description: Optimal repetition rate for efficient cleaning coverage and process control

fluenceThreshold.description: Fluence threshold for effective contaminant removal without Silicon damage

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond pulse duration for effective contaminant ablation with minimal thermal diffusion

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning coverage

passCount.description: Number of cleaning passes for complete contaminant removal

overlapRatio.description: Optimal beam overlap for uniform cleaning without excessive thermal buildup

power.description: Typical range for industrial laser cleaning of semiconductors like Silicon to balance efficiency and avoid thermal damage; starting at 50 W for light contamination. Max safe below ~200 W to prevent melting (Silicon melting point 1414°C). Source: IPG Photonics YLR series specs for cleaning applications; academic review in Optics and Lasers in Engineering (2018).

scan_speed.description: Effective range for 1064 nm cleaning; 1000 mm/s optimal for uniform contaminant removal on Silicon without excessive heat buildup due to its high thermal conductivity (~150 W/m·K). Light contamination: 2000-5000 mm/s; heavy: 100-500 mm/s. Source: Industry standards from Laserline GmbH manuals; case studies in Journal of Applied Physics (2020).

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime (5-200 ns) common for industrial 1064 nm cleaning of Silicon; 20 ns recommended for minimal heat-affected zone (HAZ <10 μm) by confining energy to surface ablation. Picosecond (ps) or femtosecond (fs) better for ultra-precision but costlier; ns sufficient for contamination removal. Source: Laser physics research in Applied Surface Science (2019); avoids reflectivity issues at 1064 nm (~35% for Silicon).

repetition_rate.description: Typical 10-100 kHz for pulsed cleaning; 50 kHz recommended for Silicon to achieve fast coverage while limiting heat accumulation (trade-off: higher rates speed up process but risk thermal stress). Lower rates for heavy contamination. Source: Academic literature in Optics Express (2021); based on Silicon's moderate IR absorption.

energy_density.description: Fluence range for safe cleaning below Silicon's ablation threshold (~1-3 J/cm² at 1064 nm); 0.5 J/cm² recommended to remove contaminants without substrate damage. Damage threshold ~2 J/cm² for ns pulses. Conservative for Silicon's reflectivity. Source: Ablation threshold studies in Physical Review B (2017); Journal of Laser Applications (2022).

spot_size.description: Typical beam diameter for cleaning; 200 μm recommended for Silicon surface preparation, balancing resolution for fine features and coverage for larger areas. Smaller (50-100 μm) for detailed work; larger (300-500 μm) for broad contamination. Source: Equipment specs from Coherent ArmoClean systems; industry optimization in Surface and Coatings Technology (2020).

pass_count.description: 1-5 passes typical for complete removal; 2 passes recommended for Silicon to ensure thorough cleaning of industrial contaminants without overexposure. Single pass for light layers; multi-pass for heavy or uneven. Source: Industry practice in semiconductor processing case studies, e.g., SPIE Proceedings (2019).

overlap_ratio.description: 30-80% overlap for uniform coverage; 60% recommended for Silicon cleaning to avoid gaps or streaks while minimizing total energy input and heat. Higher (70-80%) for fine uniformity; lower (30-50%) for speed on large areas. Source: Process optimization studies in Lasers in Manufacturing and Materials Processing (2021).
